preFix.title: Replacing a shower head and hose
preFix.subtitle: Unscrew the old handset and hose and fit new ones without isolating the water supply
preFix.excerpt: How to replace a calcified or damaged shower head and hose. Covers disconnecting the old hose, choosing a compatible replacement, and fitting without leaks.

content.text: A shower head or hose can be replaced without touching the water supply. The connections are threaded and hand-tight, the only tools needed are an adjustable spanner and fresh washers.

Almost all domestic shower hoses use a 1/2-inch BSP thread. The old hose will tell you what is fitted, unscrew one end and look at the thread. A replacement labelled '1/2 BSP' or 'standard connection' will fit.

Unscrew the lower end of the hose from the water outlet on the shower or mixer. Grip the connector with the adjustable spanner, hold the mixer body steady with one hand to avoid straining the tap body connections. Turn anti-clockwise. Have a cloth and a bowl ready for the water in the hose. Unscrew the upper end from the handset in the same way.
Step 3, Inspect and renew the washers

inside the hose end connectors. Old washers are flattened, cracked, or stiff, they are the most common source of leaks. Replace both washers from the spares bag supplied with a new hose, or with standard 1/2-inch fibre washers from a plumber's merchant.
Step 4, Fit the new hose and head

Thread the new hose onto the mixer outlet and handset by hand first. Tighten with the spanner to firm, one firm quarter-turn past hand-tight. Do not apply PTFE tape to the threads on a rubber-washer connection; the washer seals the joint. PTFE would compress the washer unevenly and can cause a leak. Restore the water supply and check for drips at both ends.

Blocked rather than damaged head: descale before replacing. Soak the head in white vinegar for 4 hours; most mineral deposits dissolve. Replace only if the head is cracked or the diverter button is broken.
Fixed overhead shower head (not a handset): connect the new head directly to the wall arm. Same BSP thread; same washer-seal principle.

items.fix: Turn off supply, remove the connection, check and replace the washer, refit and tighten.
items.cause: Washer missing, cracked, or not seated flat in the connector

items.fix: The restrictor is a small plastic disc inside the hose connector at the head end. Remove and discard it if flow is inadequate, this is done by removing the head from the hose and pressing the disc out with a thin implement.
items.cause: The new head has a flow restrictor fitted

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
